**Hardware Setup & Validation**

| **Component** | **Details** |
| --- | --- |
| Operating System | Windows 11/Ubuntu 24.04 (WSL) |
| IDE | Freedom Studio |
| CPU | _Intel i5-9300H_ |
| RAM | _8GB_ |
| Board | VSDSquadron PRO |

**Day 1 - Development Environment Setup**

**Installed Tools (Needed in Ubuntu NOT for Windows)**

| **Tool** | **Version** |
| --- | --- |
| RISC-V GCC Toolchain | ![](data:image/png;base64,) |
| OpenOCD | ![](data:image/png;base64,) |
| Serial Tool | ![](data:image/png;base64,) |
| USB Driver | libusb-win32 (via Zadig) |

**Note:** For Windows 11, **only Freedom-Studio and Zadig** will suffice.

## Installation Steps (For Windows 11 installation)

### 1\. Install USB Driver

- Download **Zadig**
- Open Zadig
- Select:
  - 1. **Options → List All Devices**
    - **Device → Dual RS-232-HS (Interface 0)**
    - **Driver → libusb-win32**
    - Click **Install/Reinstall Driver**

**![](data:image/png;base64,)**

### 2\. Install Freedom Studio

- Download the **VSDSquadron PRO Freedom Studio package (link:** <https://vsd-labs.sgp1.cdn.digitaloceanspaces.com/vsd-labs/VSDSquadronPRO.tar.gz>**)**
- Extract:

**VSDSquadronPRO.tar.gz**

- Open the extracted folder

![](data:image/png;base64,)

- Run:

**FreedomStudio-3-1-1**

![](data:image/png;base64,)

### Launch IDE

- Select a **workspace folder**
- Click **Launch**

![](data:image/png;base64,)

## Verification Commands (For Ubuntu)

### Check RISC-V Toolchain

**riscv64-unknown-elf-gcc --version**

Example output:

### ![](data:image/png;base64,)

### Check OpenOCD

**openocd --version**

Example output:

![](data:image/png;base64,)

### Serial Port Detection

When the board is connected:

**Device Manager → Ports (COM & LPT)**

Example:

**COM3 - Dual RS232-HS Interface**

**Day 2 - Program Upload & Hardware Validation**

## Example Program (For Windows installation)

Example used: **sifive-welcome**

**Expected results**: This program prints **SiFive** to the terminal and blinks the onboard LED.

- After launching Freedom-Studio, create a new project by following the steps in screenshots below.  
    ![](data:image/png;base64,)
- Once you click the "Finish" button from above step, you would see the "Debug Configuration" window.
- Connect VSDSquadron PRO board to your laptop, click on "OpenOCD" tab and then click on "Debug". Refer the screenshot below for more details:

![](data:image/png;base64,)

- Next a debug window pops up, click on the "Run" button (Step 2 in the screenshot below)

![](data:image/png;base64,)

- Next, if a windows prompt appears asking to close an active OpenOCD session, go ahead and close it by selecting "yes". Observe the example code output on the "COM" terminal of Freedom Studio.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

![C:\Users\Ansh Bharadwaj\AppData\Local\Microsoft\Windows\INetCache\Content.Word\WhatsApp Image 2026-03-05 at 23.30.26.jpeg](data:image/jpeg;base64,)

**Observable output on COM6 and User (Blue) LED blinking on the board**

**Day 3 - Board Study & Documentation**

**Hardware Specifications**

| **Feature** | **Specification** |
| --- | --- |
| Processor | SiFive FE310-G002 |
| Architecture | RV32IMAC |
| Clock Speed | 320 MHz |
| Flash Memory | 32 Mbit SPI Flash |
| SRAM | 16KB Instruction + 16KB Data |
| IO Voltage | 3.3V |

**Peripherals**

| **Peripheral** | **Details** |
| --- | --- |
| UART | 2   |
| SPI | 1   |
| I2C | 1   |
| PWM | 9   |
| GPIO | 19  |

# Board Overview

The **VSDSquadron PRO development board** is an educational embedded systems platform designed to provide hands-on experience with the **RISC-V instruction set architecture (ISA)** and modern embedded system design. The board is built around the **SiFive FE310-G002 microcontroller**, which implements a **32-bit RV32IMAC RISC-V processor core** optimized for low-power embedded and IoT applications.

This platform enables students and engineers to experiment with hardware-software interaction, peripheral communication, and real-time embedded programming.

## Processor Architecture

The board uses the **SiFive Essential™ E3 core complex**, which implements the **RV32IMAC instruction set**. This includes:

- **RV32I** - Base integer instruction set
- **M** - Hardware multiply and divide instructions
- **A** - Atomic instructions for synchronization
- **C** - Compressed instruction extension for reduced code size

The processor operates at a clock frequency of **up to 320 MHz**, enabling efficient execution of embedded workloads such as sensor processing, communication protocols, and lightweight edge-AI algorithms.

## Memory Architecture

The VSDSquadron PRO board combines on-chip memory resources with external flash storage to support embedded program execution.

### On-Chip Memory

The FE310 microcontroller provides:

- **16 KB L1 Instruction Cache**
- **16 KB Data SRAM scratchpad**

This memory structure allows fast access to frequently executed instructions and data, improving program performance and efficiency.

### External Flash

The board includes:

- **32 Mbit external SPI flash memory**

This memory stores firmware, compiled programs, and other persistent data required during system operation.

## Clocking System

The board includes an **on-board 16 MHz crystal oscillator**, which is used as the primary clock source for the microcontroller. Internal PLLs and clock management circuitry allow flexible generation of higher system frequencies required for processor operation.

This clock configuration enables stable operation and supports timing-sensitive peripherals.

## Peripheral Interfaces

The FE310 microcontroller provides several integrated peripherals that allow the board to communicate with external devices.

### UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter)

The board includes **two UART interfaces** which enable:

- serial communication with a host computer
- debugging and program output
- communication with other microcontrollers

Example pins:

- UART0 → IO16 (RX), IO17 (TX)
- UART1 → IO23 (RX), IO18 (TX)

UART is commonly used for **console output and debugging messages**.

### SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface)

The board includes a **SPI controller** that supports communication with high-speed peripherals such as:

- external flash memory
- sensors
- display controllers

Typical SPI signals include:

- MOSI
- MISO
- SCK
- Chip Select

### I2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit)

The board also includes an **I2C controller**, which is commonly used to interface with:

- sensors
- ADC/DAC modules
- EEPROM devices
- display modules

Example pins:

- SDA → IO12
- SCL → IO13

### GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output)

The board exposes **19 digital I/O pins**, which can be configured for:

- digital input
- digital output
- external interrupts
- communication protocols

GPIO pins allow the board to interact with LEDs, buttons, sensors, and other digital devices.

### PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)

The board provides **9 PWM pins** that enable generation of pulse-width modulated signals. PWM is commonly used for:

- motor control
- LED brightness control
- signal generation

### Power System

The VSDSquadron PRO board is powered through a **USB-C connector**, which provides both:

- power supply
- programming/debugging connection

The board operates at:

- **5V input voltage**
- **3.3V logic level**

Voltage regulation is handled by onboard **3.3V and 1.8V LDO regulators** to supply stable power to the processor and peripherals.

## Onboard Components

Several onboard components help users interact with the board:

- **User LED** for simple output experiments
- **Reset button** to restart the system
- **Wake button** for low-power mode experiments
- **GPIO headers** for connecting external devices

These components make the board suitable for educational experiments and embedded system prototyping.

## Applications

The VSDSquadron PRO platform is suitable for experimenting with:

- Embedded systems programming
- Internet of Things (IoT) applications
- Sensor data acquisition
- Edge computing
- Low-power system design
- RISC-V architecture learning

The combination of a modern open-source instruction set architecture and accessible hardware makes the board an excellent platform for studying both **hardware architecture and embedded software development**.

# Conclusion

In this task:

- The RISC-V development environment was successfully installed
- The VSDSquadron PRO board was connected and detected
- A sample program was compiled and executed
- UART output and LED blinking confirmed successful hardware validation

This setup establishes the foundation for further **RISC-V development and embedded system experimentation**.
